skill.name: pdb-database
skill.description: Access RCSB Protein Data Bank resources for 3D protein and nucleic acid structures, including search, metadata retrieval, and coordinate downloads.
skill.summary: Search, retrieve, and download public RCSB PDB structure data.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review found they are mostly documentation false positives or expected access to public RCSB PDB services. The real residual risks are low: examples perform public network requests, save downloaded structure files, and include one hardcoded curl subprocess debugging snippet. No evidence found of credential theft, obfuscation, prompt injection, malicious endpoints, or user-controlled command execution.

low_findings.title: Expected Public RCSB Network Access
low_findings.description: The skill documents requests to public RCSB PDB endpoints for search, metadata retrieval, and coordinate downloads. This is expected for the skill, but users should know it requires internet access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls and hardcoded URLs are directly visible and point to RCSB PDB services. No evidence shows credential exfiltration, hidden endpoints, or unrelated third-party destinations.

low_findings.title: Documented Local File Writes for Structure Downloads
low_findings.description: Example code writes downloaded PDB and mmCIF files to local paths. This is normal for coordinate downloads, but callers should validate PDB IDs and output directories in production code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and serve the documented purpose of saving structure files. Risk is limited because the examples use known PDB IDs, although the helper accepts output_dir and pdb_id parameters.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Curl Subprocess Example
low_findings.description: The reference includes a debugging example that runs curl through subprocess.run against a fixed RCSB URL. It is not command injection because command arguments are fixed and no user input is interpolated.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is present, but it uses a fixed argv list and a public RCSB endpoint. I found no shell=True usage or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command alerts are caused by markdown code fences and inline code in SKILL.md and the API reference. These are documentation delimiters, not Ruby shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown examples and code fences in documentation. They are not executable Ruby source and do not create shell execution behavior by themselves.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives in Scientific Documentation
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and weak cryptography alerts are false positives. The cited content is scientific documentation, amino acid sequences, API text, or markdown code formatting, not credential access or cryptographic logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are in a structural biology skill and contain protein sequence text, API documentation, or markdown examples. I found no evidence of Windows credential access, cryptographic operations, or malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Curl Subprocess Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference includes a debugging example that runs curl through subprocess.run against a fixed RCSB URL. It is not command injection because command arguments are fixed and no user input is interpolated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is present, but it uses a fixed argv list and a public RCSB endpoint. I found no shell=True usage or user-controlled command construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Local File Writes for Structure Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code writes downloaded PDB and mmCIF files to local paths. This is normal for coordinate downloads, but callers should validate PDB IDs and output directories in production code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and serve the documented purpose of saving structure files. Risk is limited because the examples use known PDB IDs, although the helper accepts output_dir and pdb_id parameters.

content.value_statement: Researchers need reliable access to 3D macromolecular structure data. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code search RCSB PDB, retrieve metadata, and download coordinate files.
actual_capabilities: Search RCSB PDB entries by text, attributes, sequence similarity, or structure similarity.
Retrieve entry metadata such as title, method, resolution, organism, and release dates.
Build GraphQL and REST API queries for PDB data retrieval.
Download coordinate files in PDB, mmCIF, BinaryCIF, and biological assembly formats.
Support batch workflows for multiple PDB IDs with rate-limit guidance.
Explain PDB concepts such as entities, assemblies, resolution, and mmCIF.
limitations: Requires internet access to RCSB PDB and related public API endpoints.
Does not validate biological conclusions without expert review.
Downloaded structures may require BioPython or visualization tools for deeper analysis.
High-volume workflows must handle API rate limits and caching.

use_cases.title: Retrieve structures for analysis
use_cases.description: Find high-quality PDB entries, compare experimental methods, and download coordinate files for local analysis.

use_cases.title: Find ligand-bound targets
use_cases.description: Search for protein-ligand complexes and retrieve metadata that supports binding site review.

use_cases.title: Prepare teaching examples
use_cases.description: Collect representative structures and explain PDB formats, assemblies, sequences, and resolution metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Find one structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner search for a known protein
prompt_templates.prompt: Find a PDB structure for human hemoglobin and summarize its method, resolution, and biological assembly.

prompt_templates.title: Compare structure quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Find human kinase structures with resolution better than 2.0 Angstroms and compare their experimental methods.

prompt_templates.title: Search by sequence
prompt_templates.scenario: Identify similar structures from a protein sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Search RCSB PDB for structures similar to this protein sequence and report the best matches with identity thresholds.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a batch workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced retrieval for multiple PDB IDs
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a rate-limited workflow to download mmCIF files and metadata for 4HHB, 1MBN, and 1GZX.

output_examples.output: The skill can return a short ranked list of hemoglobin entries with resolution, method, organism, and recommended download format.

output_examples.input: Compare PDB and mmCIF downloads for 4HHB.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that PDB is the legacy coordinate format, while mmCIF is preferred for richer metadata and larger structures.

output_examples.input: Plan a batch metadata search for kinase structures.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines search filters, GraphQL fields, rate-limit handling, caching, and a review checklist for returned entries.

best_practices: Use search filters first, then fetch detailed metadata for only the returned PDB IDs.
Prefer mmCIF for new analysis because it stores richer structure metadata than legacy PDB files.
Cache repeated queries and use delays or backoff for batch requests.
anti_patterns: Downloading large sets of structures before narrowing the search criteria.
Treating resolution as the only quality metric for every experimental method.
Writing downloaded files to unreviewed paths in automated workflows.

faq.question: What database does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses the public RCSB Protein Data Bank and related RCSB APIs for structure data.

faq.question: Can it download coordinate files?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents downloads for PDB, mmCIF, BinaryCIF, biological assemblies, and related structure files.

faq.question: Does it perform molecular modeling?
faq.answer: No. It helps find and retrieve existing structures, but modeling requires separate tools.

faq.question: Can it search by sequence?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes sequence similarity searches using the RCSB API client and similarity thresholds.

faq.question: Does it require credentials?
faq.answer: No credentials are documented for the public RCSB endpoints used by this skill.

faq.question: What should users verify after retrieval?
faq.answer: Users should verify experimental method, resolution, organism, ligands, assembly choice, and data quality before analysis.
